Missing the mark. You're moving forward. You're doing big deals. I've been talking a lot about debt and how much I love to use debt. But remember, you don't want to use too much debt. Do not be tempted. For some of you weak-minded, don't use too much debt. If it's too easy, too good to be true, you might want to walk away. When you use too much debt, at some point, you'll likely hit a wall, a wall where you can't service the debt. And even when the property can't service the debt, you're losing the property. If you finance a property to the gills, very difficult to do today, but you would have to do it only because you had a main first loan on the property, and then on the side, the other 20 or 25 or 30 percent you borrowed from investors are hard money lenders. And you quietly and secretly didn't let the first lender know. And if somehow tweaked a deal where almost 100 percent of the money raised was used to buy the property. In 2008, my friends and peers, they did this. They were so proud of what they were doing. I'm buying a Ralph. I'm buying a Ralph. I never put any money down. I get it all. They lost their businesses. They got stressed out. They filed bankruptcy. They lost their reputation. They were criminals. Okay? Everybody that played this game back in 2008 overleveraged their real estate, took on more debt than the real estate could pay for. Speculated on a future without the knowledge because they were greedy. They were criminal. If this happened to you, sorry, if the shoe fits, if the shoe fits, and it kicks you in the ass, learn from it. In 2008, my friends and peers, every one of them, except for one other guy, lost their businesses. The other guy and myself were invested with apartment buildings that had positive cash flow and were not overleveraged because they overleveraged and they speculated because they took a shortcut. They lost everything. I only use 50 to 75% debt on my deals. If I love a deal and I have to put as much as 50% down to own it, I will. I either love it or I don't. What I do is I figure out how to get the cash necessary to pay for the rest of the deal. If a deal is 50 million and I expect to personally put $25 million down to buy that deal, that automatically excludes most buyers and puts me and my investment in a position. So when I hear about a deal, for whatever reason, hey, Grant, on this deal, you're going to have to put 50 or 55% down to get it. I know immediately this is an opportunity. Remember, all problems are opportunities. As long as I can solve the problem, I get the payday because nobody else can buy that deal. Maybe that deal needs to be negotiated lower on price so that I don't have to put a full 50% down. Suddenly, though, I own something that most people cannot buy, which makes the asset I just purchased more valuable when I go to sell it and for whatever reason, the problem that was holding down the ability to leverage the deal, when that disappears, maybe it wasn't rented yet. Maybe it hadn't what's called stabilized yet. And now that it's stabilized, I can bring it back to the market and make a lot of money on it. That said, putting too much money down on a deal may mean that the deal is overpriced. There has to be a reason to do that. Debt should be a critical part of your analysis and your business as long as it's kept in check. Okay, let's talk about mistake number 10, buying on price and cap rate. Too often, new investors focus on finding the highest cap rates and the lowest price as possible. Now, it sounds good on paper. You sound really intelligent. I'm buying an eight cap. I'm buying low and I'm selling high. Truth is, you're not buying anything. And if you are, you're buying garbage. I know it sounds good that you can spout off that you bought at the lowest price and that you bought at an eight or nine or ten cap rate, but you're not buying deals. These are not great deals. It sounds good on paper, but if you do that, you'll never get the great deals. Great deals always come at a premium and always come at a lower cap rate. The better the neighborhood, the better the cap rate. The higher the price, the lower the cap rate. The better the product, the lower the cap rate. The higher the cap rate, the more risk, the uglier the property, the worse the neighborhood in most cases. Now, it sounds counterintuitive, but I look for deals with lower cap rates. I'm actually known as someone in the market that will pay extra to get a deal. But what deal am I getting? Remember the first goal, you got to go wide to see the deals. If people think that you're a buyer, if they think you're a target, if they think you'll pay extra, you get to look at deals that other people can't look at. If you're known as the guy that buys the highest cap rate at the lowest price, nobody's going to call you. Nobody wants to do business with you. The best deals I've ever done cost me extra. The best deals I've ever done had a lower cap rate. The best deals I've ever done, I had to pay a premium to get. I remember there was a deal I bought in Austin, Texas. It had been on the market. It had been on the market a long time. When I called the broker, I said, hey, what's the number? Grant, if you don't give asking, they won't even entertain a counter. I knew immediately that I was going to steal this deal. This broker was telling everyone that and scaring people off. I said to him, Charles, I have no problem paying the asking. Seems like a fair price to me. When can I get an LOI to you? When can I get a PSA agreed on? When can I start my due diligence? We closed quickly. I sold it four months later for a 115% return in under six months. By the time it closed, I had earned 115% on my money in six months for an 18% per month return. That's just one example. I was the top bidder. That's just one example. Another time I was the top bidder on a portfolio. I may have mentioned this earlier. 38 other qualified buyers. I outbid everybody. Based on the level of interest, 38 qualified buyers from all over the United States, I knew the day I closed that deal, I could sell it the next day. To who? One of the 37 that did not buy the deal. I knew I had a great deal on the hook. I paid 58 million on the portfolio. I did extremely conservative underwriting. This was five years ago. I thought, I told Elena, my wife, I'm going to make 20 million bucks on this deal. We could sell that deal today. This was five years ago. I paid 58. We could sell that deal today for $180 million. That's $122 million return. By the way, I only put $15 million down on the deal. $15 million of money down turned into 100. I don't even know how to say these numbers. They're so big. $122 million. Almost a 10x return. And I guarantee you, I guarantee you, I could steal today at $180 million. At $122 million profit, I could still call one of the 38 other buyers, or 37 other buyers, and I guarantee you, one of those 37 would pay me the price today because it would still be a good deal for them going forward into the future. Mistake number 11. Buying deals no one else is competing on. Look, if no one else wants it, you might want to consider not taking it home with you. Sure, it's possible that you spotted some diamond in the rough, but it's also possible that you've stumbled on a fake diamond, something that's a problem, something everyone else has realized before you is an issue. Now, that being said, there is an exception to that rule just because everybody's overlooked. It doesn't mean you're not right. This works both ways. Remember, is it a problem? Is it an opportunity? You also don't want to overdo in an over saturated market where everybody's running into that market. When you see cranes in the air and builders everywhere, beware. Cranes in the air, builders are everywhere. Optimism is overflowing. Be very careful. That are the first signs and indications that this market's about to pull back. If everyone wants in, give it a second. Do a lot of research. Do extra research. And also see, hey, this new product, this new inventory that's coming onto the market, what does it mean for the product or the apartment building that you're buying? You don't want competition like that. And if you're going to take on the competition, know how it affects you. You definitely don't need to have a battle for tenants and future buyers. Mistake number 12 is going to sound like gibberish to you, but not using a broker is a mistake. You should use a broker. Find a qualified, reputable broker. Do not try to buy a deal without a commercial broker. In the apartment space, you're going to pay a half, 1%, 1.5, maybe 2% fee for a broker. A good broker can be your best friend, your ally. To this day, I use a broker on every deal and prefer to even use brokers when the deal is off market and not listed. The more money the broker has on the line to make the better. They help me negotiate my deal. I recently locked down a deal in Houston, Texas. I might have mentioned it earlier. This deal was off market. I hired a broker outside of the Houston market, a friend of mine in Miami. I paid him $250,000. That's a quarter of a million dollars to help me get the deal done. Why? Because I need a buffer. I need glue between me and the seller in case things go emotional or go awry. I took on the loan, a whopping loan of $63 million for 500 units in the heart of Houston where the average tenant makes five times the rent. Why is that important? Because the renter, this is telling you, when the renter can pay five times the amount, it tells me I can either raise the rent. I have a very qualified renter or I'm in a great neighborhood or all of above. If we hold this asset long enough, I know we'll double our money. I have about $30 million in that deal. If things go as I believe, I'm going to earn 6 or 7% a year there and make $30 million. I don't need to worry about $250,000. I paid a broker. I happily wrote the check. I thanked him, I bought him dinner and I patted him on the back and said, hey, I couldn't have done that deal without you. Always use a broker.
